interpretation_summary: The replication followed the preregistration plan using an independent-samples t-test on the Prolific dataset (n≈474) to compare 1D test-phase scores between contingent-information and full-information conditions. Execution produced the planned outputs (analysis.nb.html, analysis-derived.csv). Results mirrored the original study’s direction and magnitude: contingent > full-information by about 0.073 (95% CI [0.044, 0.102], p<0.001), consistent with the original’s reported difference of ~0.08 with a 95% posterior credible interval excluding zero. Overall, the replication supports the focal claim with high fidelity.

fidelity_assessment.method_alignment: Full alignment: Independent-samples t-test (equal variances) on acc_1d computed from test-phase trials, comparing contingent vs full-information as preregistered; derived dataset already reflected analytic exclusions; outputs saved as planned.

deviations.issue_description: Original study reported Bayesian credible intervals, whereas the replication used a frequentist t-test and 95% confidence interval.
deviations.impact: Low: Preregistered success criterion was direction and 95% CI excluding zero; the replication met this criterion.

deviations.issue_description: Minor execution adjustment: initial wrapper treated an Rmd as a shell script; replaced with a bash script that calls rmarkdown::render.
deviations.impact: None: Analysis ran to completion and produced the planned outputs.

results_comparison.hypothesis_tested: Participants in the contingent-information condition will have a higher one-dimensional (1D) strategy score at test than participants in the full-information condition.
results_comparison.original_results: Experiment 1 (bee task): Mean 1D score contingent = 0.83; full-information = 0.75; 95% posterior credible interval for the mean difference = [0.03, 0.14], excluding zero (direction: contingent > full).
results_comparison.replication_results: Prolific replication (n≈474): Mean 1D accuracy contingent = 0.7708 (SE = 0.0117; ~95% CI [0.748, 0.794]); full-information = 0.6974 (SE = 0.0088; ~95% CI [0.680, 0.714]); mean difference = 0.0733 with 95% CI [0.044, 0.102]; t-test p < 0.001; Cohen’s d = 0.457 (direction: contingent > full).
results_comparison.overall_answer: Yes. The replication results satisfy the preregistered comparison criteria for the focal claim: direction matches (contingent > full) and the 95% CI excludes zero.

replication_report: Replication successful: Contingent-information led to higher 1D test scores than full-information by ~0.073 (95% CI [0.044, 0.102], p<0.001; d≈0.46), consistent in direction and similar in magnitude to the original (.83 vs .75; credible interval excluding zero).

failure_handling.suggestions: Provide a dedicated shell entrypoint script (e.g., analysis.sh) rather than an Rmd wrapper; include a Makefile or Rscript entry to call rmarkdown::render to reduce orchestration friction.
For closer comparability to the original, add a Bayesian estimation step (e.g., using rstanarm or brms) to report posterior credible intervals alongside the t-test CI.

notes: The replication used an equal-variances t-test as planned; reporting a Welch test as a sensitivity check would further strengthen robustness. Differences in participant pool (Prolific vs MTurk) and larger N likely contributed to narrower confidence intervals. The point estimate of the difference (~0.073) is close to the original (~0.08). Future work could also report analogous 2D scores and Bayesian intervals for full comparability with the original reporting style.
